---
title: "Beautiful Grass-Veneer (Crambus satrapellus)"
slug: "crambus-satrapellus"
group: "Insecta"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/475761683/medium.jpeg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Beautiful Grass-Veneer"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Animal"
tax_class: "Insecta"
tax_order: "Lepidoptera"
tax_family: "Crambidae"
categories: ["Animal", "Insecta", "Lepidoptera", "Crambidae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/475761683/medium.jpeg" alt="Beautiful Grass-Veneer" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/shelleydee">
shelleydee</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/264918280">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Insecta
-   **Order:** Lepidoptera
-   **Family:** Crambidae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=144074)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not available
-   **Rarity note:** Uncommon across Florida.

## Natural history notes

Small, slender moth characterized by its silvery-white forewings marked with longitudinal streaks and a subtle sheen. In Florida, it is found in grassy habitats such as lawns, fields, and open areas where its larvae feed on grasses. Adults are active throughout much of the year in warmer regions and are often flushed from vegetation during the day or attracted to light at night.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/144074-Crambus-satrapellus)
-   [Local profile](https://matthewwills.com/2020/06/18/discovery-week-iii/)
